questions.q: The Phong lighting model combines which three components to calculate surface color?
options: Diffuse, specular, and ambient
Emissive, reflective, and transmissive
Direct and indirect illumination
Shadows, reflections, and refractions
questions.explanation: Phong lighting calculates final color as the sum of ambient (global illumination), diffuse (matte surface reflection), and specular (shiny highlights) components. This model is computationally efficient for real-time graphics.

questions.q: What does the normal vector represent in lighting calculations?
options: The direction of reflected light
The direction perpendicular to the surface
The direction toward the light source
The direction the surface is moving
questions.explanation: The normal vector is perpendicular to the surface. It determines how much light hits the surface based on the angle between the normal and light direction (dot product).

questions.q: In the Phong model, which component creates sharp, bright highlights on shiny surfaces?
options: Ambient lighting
Diffuse reflection
Specular reflection
Texture mapping
questions.explanation: Specular reflection creates the bright, focused highlights seen on shiny surfaces. Diffuse provides matte coloring; ambient provides global background illumination without direction.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the shininess exponent in the Phong specular calculation?
options: Controls the brightness of the specular highlight
Controls the size and sharpness of the specular highlight
Determines the surface color
Adjusts the ambient light intensity
questions.explanation: The shininess exponent (power) controls how concentrated the specular highlight is. Higher exponents produce smaller, sharper highlights; lower exponents create broader, duller highlights.

questions.q: What does a BRDF (Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function) describe?
options: How light bounces between surfaces
How much light a surface reflects in any given direction based on incoming direction
The brightness of a point light source
The color of ambient illumination
questions.explanation: A BRDF characterizes how a surface reflects light in all directions given an incoming light direction. It's more general than Phong and forms the basis of physically-based rendering.

questions.q: Why must normal vectors be normalized before use in lighting calculations?
options: To match the scale of the model matrix
To ensure the dot product with light direction produces correct intensity
To improve rendering speed
To avoid division by zero errors
questions.explanation: Normalized normals ensure that the dot product (cos of angle) correctly represents the lighting intensity. Non-normalized normals produce incorrect brightness values proportional to their length.

questions.q: In per-vertex lighting (Gouraud shading), what is interpolated across the triangle?
options: Normal vectors
Light colors calculated at each vertex
Texture coordinates
Depth values
questions.explanation: Gouraud shading calculates lighting at vertices and interpolates the resulting colors across the triangle. This is less expensive than per-pixel lighting but can produce artifacts on curved surfaces.

questions.q: What is a key limitation of the Phong lighting model compared to physically-based approaches?
options: It cannot handle multiple light sources
It does not conserve energy and can produce unrealistic reflections
It requires too much computation for real-time rendering
It cannot represent metallic surfaces
questions.explanation: Phong is not energy-conserving, meaning it can reflect more light than it receives. Physically-based rendering ensures specular plus diffuse reflection respects energy conservation.

questions.q: How does the ambient lighting component in Phong shading model global illumination?
options: By tracing rays to all light sources
By using a constant color representing approximate indirect light
By blurring shadows across the surface
By computing light bounces between surfaces
questions.explanation: Ambient lighting in Phong is a simple constant approximation of indirect illumination from all directions. It does not simulate actual global illumination; physically-based methods like ray tracing do that.

questions.q: In normal mapping, what technique allows surface details without increasing geometry complexity?
options: Using pre-calculated shadows in textures
Storing perturbed normal vectors in a texture to simulate bumps and dents
Reducing polygon count with texture blending
Caching lighting calculations in a lookup table
questions.explanation: Normal mapping stores modified normal vectors in a texture, perturbing them per-pixel to simulate surface detail without additional geometry. This creates the illusion of bumps and creases efficiently.
